The Daily Graph¶

Introduction and summary.

The spikes continue, particularly in Leon and Fulton counties.¶

Some annecdotal data on testing in Leon County. FSU reports that it has tested 3,000 people, 115 (3.8%) were positive.¶

          07/17/2020

The last 7 days¶

The following table displays the number of new cases for the lastest 7 days of available data in each of the counties. Zero, when it occurs, probably means a missing day's data since I calculate new cases based on the diffence in cumulative number between successive days.

Date Campbell Fulton Hamilton Leon
7/10/20 7 435 167 114
7/11/20 9 333 218 85
7/12/20 7 241 169 102
7/13/20 3 485 96 100
7/14/20 9 274 133 72
7/15/20 1 392 100 86
7/16/20 8 438 136 103

Examine new cases, moving averages

The moving averages are normalized as the number of cases per 100,000 population. As noted before, the averages are 'trailing' in that the point on the graph represents the average number of new confirmed cases for that day and the $k-1$ previous days ($k = 5,7,10$).

A comparison of moving averages amongst the counties.¶

A comparison of the different moving averages within counties.¶

Cumulative Number of cases by county.¶

Log rate of occurrence
I left all counties on the same graph for now since the log scale compensates a bit for the rather large differences. Recall that if the occurrence was exponential, the semi-log graph would be linear. This doesn't occur in any of the counties. As the above graph demonstrated, after about 30 days, Hamilton and Fulton counties are extremely similar.
